Following completion of confirmatory diligence, selected parties will be invited to submit final binding proposals. The final bid deadline will be communicated during Phase II.

The Company\'s counsel, Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP, will distribute a draft purchase agreement --- expected to be structured as a stock purchase agreement --- to Phase III participants. **[III. IOI Submission Requirements]{.underline}**

Each IOI should be in letter format, addressed to Philip Tran at Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC, and should include the following:

8\. **Contact Information.** Primary point of contact for the bidder and its legal and financial advisors.

**CONFIDENTIAL --- Internal Memorandum**

**Environmental Services Sector Overview and CES Opportunity Screening**

**From:** Sarah Lindgren, Vice President, Thornfield Capital Partners

**To:** Marcus Yuen, Partner (Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook, Managing Partner

**CC:** David Koh, Associate

**Date:** June 12, 2025

**Re:** Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. --- Sector Context and Preliminary Screening Notes

Prepared in connection with the Confidential Information Memorandum received from Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC on June 9, 2025. IOI deadline: July 14, 2025.

**[1. Purpose and Executive Summary]{.underline}**

This memorandum provides sector-level context to support the deal team\'s evaluation of Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (\"CES\"), a Delaware C-corporation formed in 2009 operating as an environmental services platform across the Pacific Northwest (Oregon, Washington, Idaho, and Northern California). The Confidential Information Memorandum (\"CIM\") was prepared by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC (Seattle, WA), under the direction of Managing Director Philip Tran.

CES reported FY2024 revenue of \$87.3 million across three operating segments: Environmental Remediation (\$48.2M), Industrial Cleaning (\$27.8M), and Emergency Response (\$11.3M). The company would represent an acquisition opportunity for Thornfield Capital Partners Fund III, LP (\"Fund III\"), which closed at \$425 million in 2023 and targets platforms in the \$30M--\$100M enterprise value range within industrial and environmental services end markets. CES fits squarely within these parameters.

Thornfield has meaningful prior experience in the environmental services sector through two Fund II portfolio companies: GreenWorks Remediation (acquired 2019) and Allied Waste Systems (acquired 2021). Operational and financial data from both investments are referenced throughout this memo as proprietary benchmarking sources.

This memo covers the following: (i) the broader environmental services sector landscape; (ii) PFAS regulatory developments and the realistic market opportunity for PFAS-related services; (iii) typical financial profiles and operating benchmarks for companies of CES\'s size and service mix; (iv) regulatory enforcement trends and the recurring nature of compliance costs; and (v) preliminary observations on CES\'s CIM metrics relative to sector norms.

**Key takeaway:** The environmental services sector remains fundamentally attractive for Fund III, supported by regulatory tailwinds, consolidation dynamics, and recurring revenue characteristics. However, several aspects of the CES CIM warrant careful scrutiny against sector benchmarks --- particularly the aggressiveness of PFAS revenue growth projections and the treatment of regulatory and compliance costs as fully non-recurring EBITDA adjustments. David Koh should use this memo as a reference framework when preparing the comprehensive buy-side CIM analysis.

**[2. Environmental Services Sector Landscape]{.underline}**

**2.1 Market Overview**

The U.S. environmental services market encompasses remediation, industrial cleaning, waste management, emergency response, and specialty services (including emerging contaminant categories such as PFAS). Demand is driven primarily by regulatory mandates from the federal EPA and state departments of environmental quality, as well as industrial activity levels, infrastructure investment, and growing awareness of emerging contaminants.

The Pacific Northwest sub-market is characterized by a diverse industrial base --- active refining, lumber and paper, steel production, power generation, and government agencies (notably the Oregon Department of Transportation) --- all of which are represented in CES\'s customer mix. Lower middle-market environmental services companies (\$50M--\$150M in revenue) tend to operate regionally, building durable competitive positions through local relationships, deep regulatory expertise, and specialized permits and licenses that create meaningful barriers to entry.

CES\'s competitive moat includes hazardous waste transporter permits in Oregon, Washington, Idaho, and California, as well as the Oregon DEQ Contractor License ENV-2009-04821 and licensed Professional Engineers (\"PEs\") required for project execution. These credentials take years to accumulate and are not easily replicated by new market entrants.

From a transaction perspective, PE-backed consolidation has accelerated over the past several years. Comparable transaction multiples for well-positioned environmental services platforms have moved into the 8.0x--10.0x Adjusted EBITDA range, reflecting buyer appetite for recurring revenue characteristics and regulatory-driven demand.

**2.2 Key End Markets Relevant to CES**

CES serves a representative cross-section of Pacific Northwest end markets:

**Refining and petrochemical.** This is a significant source of recurring remediation and industrial cleaning revenue across the sector. CES\'s largest customer, Pacific Northwest Refining Co. (\"PNR\"), generated \$18.7 million in FY2024 revenue, representing 21.4% of total revenue. This level of single-customer concentration is consistent with sector norms for companies of CES\'s scale but constitutes a risk factor that warrants diligence into contractual terms, relationship depth, and alternative revenue replacement capacity.

**Government/DOT.** The Oregon Department of Transportation (\"ODOT\") at \$9.1 million (10.4% of revenue) is consistent with sector norms. Government environmental services contracts are generally sticky, with predictable renewal patterns and low credit risk.

**Power/utilities.** Columbia Basin Power Authority at \$7.3 million (8.4%) under contract through December 31, 2027, is representative of the longer-term contract structures common in utility-focused environmental services.

**Forest products and metals.** Meridian Lumber & Paper (\$5.8M) and Willamette Steel Corp. (\$4.9M) expose CES to cyclical industrial end markets that introduce revenue variability, particularly during economic downturns.

Overall, CES\'s end market diversification is reasonable but weighted toward cyclical industrial customers, which should be stress-tested under downside scenarios.

**[3. PFAS Regulatory Developments and Market Opportunity]{.underline}**

**3.1 Federal and State PFAS Regulatory Backdrop**

Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (\"PFAS\") are a class of synthetic chemicals subject to intensifying regulatory scrutiny at both the federal and state levels. The EPA finalized national drinking water standards for PFAS in 2024, creating substantial demand for investigation, monitoring, and remediation services. Oregon, Washington, and other Pacific Northwest states have adopted --- or are in the process of developing --- state-level PFAS regulations that exceed federal requirements in certain respects.

State-level enforcement actions and cleanup mandates are expected to accelerate through 2025--2028, particularly for sites near airports, military installations, manufacturing facilities, and wastewater treatment plants. PFAS remediation is technically complex, requiring specialized treatment technologies (granular activated carbon, ion exchange, high-pressure oxidation) and experienced personnel with specific training and credentials.

**3.2 PFAS Market Growth: Realistic Assessment**

Industry analysts project the U.S. PFAS remediation market to grow at 25--35% annually over the next three to five years from its current nascent base. This is a genuinely attractive sector-level growth dynamic. However, the sector-wide growth rate should not be conflated with the growth rate achievable by any single company. Individual company growth in PFAS services is constrained by several factors:

> \(a\) Availability of licensed engineers and trained technicians with PFAS-specific expertise;
>
> \(b\) Capital investment required for specialized treatment and monitoring equipment;
>
> \(c\) Competitive dynamics, as larger national environmental services firms enter the PFAS market with greater resources; and
>
> \(d\) The pace of regulatory enforcement that actually triggers funded remediation work, as opposed to investigation and assessment activity.

CES launched its PFAS practice in 2022, generating \$1.1 million in initial revenue. The practice grew to \$3.4 million in FY2023 and \$6.2 million in FY2024. The early trajectory is encouraging but from a small base.

**The CIM projects PFAS revenue of \$14.0 million in FY2025E (125.8% year-over-year growth) and \$22.0 million in FY2026E (57.1% year-over-year growth).** These projections imply that PFAS drives approximately 64% of total incremental revenue growth from FY2024 (\$87.3M) to FY2026E (\$112.0M) --- that is, \$15.8 million of the \$24.7 million total revenue increase.

**This level of reliance on a two-year-old service line to underpin the platform growth story is aggressive.** Thornfield\'s direct experience at GreenWorks Remediation is instructive. GreenWorks attempted a comparable emerging-service-line expansion (vapor intrusion mitigation) with sell-side projections of 80%+ growth in years two and three. Actual growth moderated to approximately 35% annually once the practice exceeded \$5 million in revenue, as initial pent-up demand was worked through and competitive pressures intensified. Based on this experience, we would expect CES\'s PFAS practice to moderate to 30--50% annual growth now that it has crossed the \$5 million threshold.

**Key diligence question:** What is the contracted PFAS backlog supporting the \$14.0 million FY2025E projection? How much is based on signed contracts versus pipeline assumptions? The CIM does not provide this breakout, and it must be requested during the diligence process.

If PFAS growth disappoints --- say, reaching \$9--10 million in FY2025 rather than \$14 million --- the overall revenue trajectory weakens materially, and the projected margin expansion from 13.9% reported to 20.0%+ adjusted becomes difficult to achieve, as the PFAS practice is presented in the CIM as carrying above-average margins.

An additional capacity constraint deserves attention. CES\'s Professional Engineer headcount declined from three to two in January 2025 following the retirement of one PE who has not yet been replaced. PFAS projects typically require PE oversight for site assessment and remediation design. Growth at the projected pace would likely require hiring at least two to three additional PEs, and the environmental PE market is extremely tight --- typical recruitment timelines run six to twelve months. The deal team should assess whether management has a credible PE hiring plan and whether the loss of one-third of its PE capacity creates a near-term execution bottleneck.

**[4. Typical Financial Profiles of Environmental Services Companies]{.underline}**

**4.1 Revenue and Margin Benchmarks**

Lower middle-market environmental services companies (\$50M--\$150M in revenue) typically generate EBITDA margins of 10--15% on a reported basis. CES\'s reported FY2024 EBITDA of \$12.1 million on revenue of \$87.3 million yields a 13.9% margin, which is within the normal range and suggests a reasonably well-managed operation.

Adjusted EBITDA margins above 18--20% warrant scrutiny in this sector. Common adjustments in owner-operator transactions --- particularly compensation normalization and related-party transaction adjustments --- can be significant, and each adjustment should be independently verified during the QoE process. The CIM\'s Adjusted EBITDA margin of 19.6% (based on \$17.1 million of Adjusted EBITDA) is at the upper boundary of what we would consider plausible and requires careful validation.

Revenue growth in the Pacific Northwest environmental services market has generally run 5--10% annually over the past three to five years. CES\'s 12.5% revenue CAGR from FY2021 to FY2024 is above average, likely reflecting a combination of organic growth in core services and the PFAS practice ramp.

**4.2 Capital Expenditure Benchmarks**

Environmental services is a capital-intensive sector. Companies operating heavy equipment --- vacuum trucks, hydroblasting units, excavation equipment, tanker trucks --- require significant ongoing maintenance capital expenditure to keep fleets operational and in regulatory compliance.

**Typical maintenance capex for environmental services companies of CES\'s size and service mix ranges from 5--7% of revenue.** For CES at \$87.3 million in revenue, this implies maintenance capex of \$4.4 million to \$6.1 million annually.

Thornfield\'s portfolio data confirms this range. At GreenWorks Remediation, actual maintenance capex ran approximately 6.2% of revenue during the first two years of ownership, after the pre-acquisition period had understated maintenance capex to approximately 3.5% of revenue. Equipment age and deferred maintenance became apparent only after closing and conducting a detailed fleet assessment. At Allied Waste Systems, maintenance capex was approximately 5.8% of revenue over the holding period.

**The CIM characterizes CES\'s maintenance capex at \$3.0 million annually (3.4% of revenue), which is materially below the sector range of 5--7%.** The deal team should investigate whether capex has been deferred in the pre-sale period and should request a detailed fleet age and condition assessment as part of early diligence. If true maintenance capex is \$5.5 million (the midpoint of the sector range) rather than \$3.0 million, the \$2.5 million difference directly reduces free cash flow and affects the implied return.

**4.3 Working Capital and DSO Benchmarks**

Well-managed environmental services companies typically maintain days sales outstanding (\"DSO\") of 45--55 days. DSO above 60 days can indicate collection issues, customer disputes, aging receivables, or revenue recognition timing differences.

Net working capital (\"NWC\") as a percentage of revenue typically runs 10--14% for the sector. CES\'s reported NWC of \$11.8 million represents 13.5% of FY2024 revenue, which is within the normal range.

DSO should be independently verified against the balance sheet accounts receivable figure. CIM-stated metrics sometimes reflect management\'s \"normalized\" DSO rather than actual period-end calculations. Any discrepancy between stated DSO and the figure implied by the balance sheet AR balance and trailing revenue should be flagged during diligence.

**[5. Regulatory Enforcement Trends and Compliance Cost Realities]{.underline}**

**5.1 Regulatory Environment for Environmental Services Companies**

Environmental services companies are subject to extensive federal and state regulatory oversight, including the EPA, state departments of environmental quality (Oregon DEQ in CES\'s case), OSHA, DOT (for hazardous materials transportation), and state contractor licensing boards. Companies handling hazardous materials face inherent regulatory risk --- even well-run operations encounter enforcement actions, notices of violation, permit compliance issues, and site-specific regulatory disputes as a routine component of operations.

Oregon DEQ has been particularly active in enforcement over the past several years, with an increasing number of enforcement actions related to waste storage, transportation documentation, and site remediation compliance. This trend is relevant to evaluating CES\'s regulatory history and the likelihood of future compliance costs.

**5.2 Recurring Nature of Regulatory and Compliance Costs**

**A critical area for buy-side diligence in any environmental services acquisition is the treatment of regulatory and compliance costs in EBITDA adjustments.**

Recurring regulatory and compliance costs are common in this sector and typically run \$200,000 to \$500,000 annually for companies of CES\'s size (\$75M--\$100M in revenue). These costs include routine regulatory defense, permit renewals and compliance audits, environmental health and safety program maintenance, minor enforcement action responses, and periodic fines and penalties.

**It is inappropriate to treat all regulatory costs as \"one-time\" add-backs** unless the specific enforcement action is demonstrably unrelated to ongoing operations and unlikely to recur. Larger settlements (\$500K+) may have a non-recurring component, but QoE best practice is to normalize to an ongoing regulatory cost run-rate and add back only the amount in excess of that run-rate --- not the full expense.

Thornfield\'s portfolio experience is directly on point:

**GreenWorks Remediation.** The sell-side CIM added back \$380,000 for a \"one-time\" state enforcement settlement. Post-acquisition, the company incurred approximately \$275,000 in regulatory and compliance costs in each of the following two years, demonstrating that a baseline level of regulatory expense is inherent in the business model. The QoE analysis ultimately recommended adding back only the amount in excess of a \$250,000 annual run-rate.

**Allied Waste Systems.** Recurring regulatory and compliance costs averaged approximately \$310,000 annually over a four-year period, with one year spiking to \$840,000 due to an EPA investigation. The spike year was partially added back in the QoE, but a normalized run-rate of \$300,000 was maintained as an ongoing operating expense.

**Implication for CES:** The CIM adds back \$1.2 million related to the August 14, 2024 Oregon DEQ enforcement action settlement (\$875,000 settlement plus \$325,000 in associated legal fees). The deal team should evaluate the following:

> 1\. Whether CES has a history of prior enforcement actions suggesting systemic compliance issues rather than a truly isolated event;
>
> 2\. What the appropriate ongoing regulatory cost run-rate is for CES (likely \$200,000--\$500,000 annually based on sector benchmarks and Thornfield\'s portfolio data); and
>
> 3\. Whether the full \$1.2 million add-back is defensible or whether only the amount above the normalized run-rate should be added back. If a \$300,000 run-rate is appropriate, the defensible add-back would be approximately \$900,000 (\$1.2M less \$300K), not the full \$1.2 million. If the run-rate is \$500,000, the defensible add-back would be \$700,000.

This distinction may appear small in absolute terms but directly affects the Adjusted EBITDA used in valuation --- a \$300K--\$500K difference at an 8.0x--10.0x multiple translates to \$2.4M--\$5.0M in enterprise value.

**[6. Thornfield\'s Environmental Services Portfolio Experience]{.underline}**

**6.1 GreenWorks Remediation (Fund II, 2019)**

Acquired in 2019 as a soil and groundwater remediation platform in the Southeast. Key lessons applicable to CES:

> • Sell-side CIM understated maintenance capex at approximately 3.5% of revenue; actual post-acquisition maintenance capex was approximately 6.2%, driven by deferred maintenance and aging equipment that had not been properly disclosed.
>
> • A \$380K regulatory cost add-back was partially recurring. Post-close, the company incurred approximately \$275K per year in ongoing regulatory and compliance expense.
>
> • An emerging service line (vapor intrusion mitigation) grew materially slower than CIM projections after initial pent-up demand was satisfied. Management\'s case projected 80%+ growth in years two and three; actual growth was approximately 35%.
>
> • Key person risk materialized when the lead project manager departed within six months of close. Retention agreements should have been a closing condition.

GreenWorks is currently held and performing at plan after operational improvements, but the first two years post-close required significant management attention to address pre-acquisition underinvestment.

**6.2 Allied Waste Systems (Fund II, 2021)**

Acquired in 2021 as an industrial waste transportation and disposal business in the Mid-Atlantic. Key lessons:

> • Regulatory and compliance costs averaged approximately \$310K annually, with one spike year at \$840K.
>
> • Maintenance capex was approximately 5.8% of revenue.
>
> • Customer concentration was a material risk that required active diversification post-acquisition.
>
> • PE licensing requirements across multiple states created staffing bottlenecks during the growth phase.

Allied Waste is currently held and performing above plan following a successful add-on acquisition.

**6.3 Relevance to CES Evaluation**

CES shares many operational and financial characteristics with GreenWorks and Allied Waste: founder-led, regional scale, capital-intensive, and regulatory-sensitive. The lessons from both investments --- particularly regarding maintenance capex understating, recurring regulatory costs presented as non-recurring, and emerging service line growth moderation --- should directly inform how the deal team evaluates the CES CIM. Pattern recognition across these transactions strongly suggests that seller CIMs in this sector consistently understate maintenance capex and overstate the \"one-time\" nature of regulatory costs.

**[7. Preliminary Observations on CES Relative to Sector Benchmarks]{.underline}**

The following table summarizes CES CIM metrics against the sector benchmarks developed in this memo:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Metric**                         **CES (per CIM)**      **Sector Benchmark**              **Observation**
  ---------------------------------- ---------------------- --------------------------------- -----------------------------------------------------
  Revenue growth CAGR (FY21--24)     12.5%                  5--10%                            Above average; partly PFAS-driven

  Reported EBITDA margin             13.9%                  10--15%                           Within range

  CIM Adjusted EBITDA margin         19.6% (\$17.1M)        15--18% adjusted                  High; adjustments warrant scrutiny

  Maintenance capex (% of revenue)   3.4% (\$3.0M)          5--7%                             Below sector range; likely understated

  CIM-stated DSO                     52 days                45--55 days                       Appears in range; must verify against balance sheet

  Regulatory cost treatment          Full \$1.2M add-back   \$200K--\$500K ongoing run-rate   Likely overstated as non-recurring

  PFAS growth (FY24 → FY25E)         125.8%                 30--50% (company-level)           Significantly above achievable range
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Areas of Concern for Further Diligence (Prioritized):**

1\. **PFAS revenue projections and contracted backlog.** The \$14.0M FY2025E projection implies company-specific growth approximately three times the achievable rate indicated by sector data and Thornfield\'s portfolio experience. David should request a backlog breakout between signed contracts and pipeline.

2\. **Regulatory compliance history and appropriate cost run-rate.** The full \$1.2M add-back is likely overstated by \$300K--\$500K. Request CES\'s complete regulatory enforcement history for the past ten years.

3\. **Maintenance capex and fleet condition.** At 3.4% of revenue, CES\'s stated maintenance capex is 150--200 basis points below the sector floor. A detailed fleet age and condition assessment is critical.

4\. **DSO verification.** Independently calculate DSO from balance sheet accounts receivable and trailing revenue to confirm the CIM\'s stated 52-day figure.

5\. **Adjusted EBITDA adjustment defensibility.** Owner compensation normalization and the above-market lease adjustment require independent verification during QoE.

**Positive Attributes:**

> • Established platform with 15+ years of operating history under consistent founder leadership.
>
> • Diversified service offering across remediation, industrial cleaning, and emergency response, providing multiple revenue streams and cross-selling opportunities.
>
> • Attractive geographic footprint with meaningful regulatory tailwinds, particularly around PFAS and state-level environmental enforcement in Oregon and Washington.
>
> • Revenue growth track record is genuine, even applying a discount to PFAS projections.
>
> • CES fits squarely within Fund III\'s target criteria for size, sector, and geographic focus.

**Recommendation:** Proceed with detailed CIM analysis. David Koh to prepare a comprehensive buy-side CIM analysis memorandum identifying all financial inconsistencies and risk factors, using the sector benchmarks in this memo as the evaluative framework. Target completion by June 25, 2025 to allow sufficient time for team discussion and investment committee preparation ahead of the July 14, 2025 IOI deadline.

**[8. Appendix: Key Sector Data Sources and References]{.underline}**

The sector benchmarks referenced throughout this memo are derived from the following sources:

> \(a\) Thornfield\'s proprietary portfolio company data, principally from GreenWorks Remediation and Allied Waste Systems, both held in Fund II;
>
> \(b\) Industry association reports and environmental services market studies;
>
> \(c\) Public filings of environmental services companies with comparable service offerings and end market exposure; and
>
> \(d\) Thornfield\'s internal environmental services deal screening database, maintained since 2017.

All figures are approximate and should be refined during formal diligence with the assistance of Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP (buyer\'s counsel) and an independent quality of earnings provider to be selected by the deal team.

This memorandum is intended solely for internal use by Thornfield Capital Partners investment professionals and should not be shared with any external party, including Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC, Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc., or its management team.

{"file_path": "buy-side-cim-analysis-memo.md", "content": "# CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL MEMORANDUM

## Buy-Side CIM Analysis — Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. ("CES" / "Project Cascade")

| | |
|---|---|
| **To:** | Marcus Yuen (Partner, Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook (Managing Partner) |
| **From:** | David Koh (Associate) |
| **CC:** | Sarah Lindgren (Vice President) |
| **Date:** | June 25, 2025 |
| **Re:** | Buy-side analysis of the CES Confidential Information Memorandum and supporting materials — financial inconsistencies, risk factors, valuation, and IOI recommendations |
| **Process:** | IOI deadline July 14, 2025; target close Q4 2025; stock purchase structure |
| **Buyer counsel:** | Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP |

---

## 1. Executive Summary

**Recommendation: Proceed to submit a non-binding Indication of Interest, but anchor valuation on a buyer-adjusted EBITDA that is materially below the seller's $17.1 million figure, and condition the bid on a structured earnout and a defined diligence gate.** CES is a genuine, well-positioned Pacific Northwest environmental services platform that fits Fund III's sector and geographic focus, and Thornfield's GreenWorks and Allied Waste experience is directly relevant. However, the sell-side materials contain a pattern of internal inconsistencies, aggressive EBITDA adjustments, a customer-concentration contract that expires before close, and a growth thesis that is almost entirely dependent on a two-year-old service line. These are diligence-resolvable in part, but several items (data integrity across the CIM and data room, the PNR contract expiration, owner transition, and PFAS execution) are material to price and structure and should be reflected in the IOI.

**Valuation view.** The CIM implies an enterprise value of **$136.8 million–$171.0 million** (8.0x–10.0x FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1 million) and pushes the "higher end," with a forward view of $157.6 million–$197.0 million on FY2025E Adjusted EBITDA of $19.7 million. After correcting the EBITDA bridge for the issues identified in this memo, **buyer-defensible FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA is approximately $14.1 million–$15.2 million** (central case ~$14.6 million), implying an EV range of roughly **$113 million–$152 million at 8.0x–10.0x**, with a central case near **$126 million–$137 million (≈9.0x)**. I recommend an IOI enterprise-value range of approximately **$115 million–$140 million** on a cash-free, debt-free basis with normalized net working capital, with the upper portion delivered through an earnout tied to (i) PFAS revenue achievement against the $14.0 million FY2025E target and (ii) renewal of the Pacific Northwest Refining Co. ("PNR") master service agreement. Note that even the lower end of the seller's range exceeds Fund III's stated $30 million–$100 million platform EV target; fund-fit and any co-invest requirement should be confirmed with the IC before the IOI is submitted.

**Top risks (detailed in Sections 6–15):**

1. **Sell-side financials do not reconcile across documents.** The CIM's condensed income statement and balance sheet differ materially from the Financial Exhibits workbook — net income differs by $1.7 million ($5.0M CIM vs. $6.7M exhibits), total assets by $3.9 million, and the exhibits' own income statement does not internally tie (Operating Income + D&A ≠ Reported EBITDA in every year). The exhibits also reveal $2.5 million of goodwill/intangibles and a below-the-line classification of the DEQ settlement that the CIM does not surface.
2. **Adjusted EBITDA is 29% adjustments ($5.0M of $17.1M), and several add-backs are overstated.** The related-party lease add-back contradicts the CIM's own market comparable; the DEQ settlement appears to be double-counted; regulatory costs are treated as fully non-recurring against sector norms; and the owner-compensation normalization ignores transition overlap and a real replacement-CEO cost.
3. **The largest customer's contract expires before close.** PNR (21.4% of FY2024 revenue, $18.7 million) operates under an MSA expiring March 31, 2025 — six to nine months before the expected Q4 2025 close.
4. **The growth thesis is PFAS-dependent and aggressive.** PFAS is projected to grow 125.8% in FY2025 (~3x the achievable company-level rate per sector data and Thornfield's GreenWorks analog) and drives ~64% of incremental FY2024–FY2026 revenue; contracted PFAS backlog is not disclosed.
5. **Free cash flow is negligible on a normalized basis.** Reported FCF is only $2.0–$2.8 million against $12.1 million EBITDA, and if maintenance capex is normalized to the sector 5–7% range (vs. the CIM's 3.4%), FCF falls to roughly $0–$1 million. A buyer is paying a high FCF multiple for EBITDA that does not currently convert to cash.
6. **Key-person and capacity risk.** Founder/CEO/68% owner Randall Oakes (age 61) exits within 18–24 months; PE headcount just fell from three to two (January 2025), constraining the remediation and PFAS sign-off capacity that the growth plan requires.
7. **Data integrity.** The CIM and the Customer Summary & Backlog Report disagree on the identity of five of the top ten customers, the segments served by three of the top five, and the relationship start dates of four of the top five — despite identical dollar amounts and subtotals.

---

## 2. Documents Reviewed and Methodology

This memo analyzes the full Project Cascade document set distributed by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC ("Greenleaf") on June 9, 2025:

1. **Confidential Information Memorandum** ("CIM"), Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc., June 2025 (Greenleaf).
2. **Customer Summary and Backlog Report** ("Backlog Report"), June 2025 (Greenleaf).
3. **Financial Exhibits** workbook ("Exhibits"), multi-sheet Excel (Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, EBITDA Bridge, Revenue by Segment, Capex Schedule).
4. **Process Letter — Exploration of Strategic Alternatives**, June 9, 2025 (Greenleaf).
5. **Sector Overview and CES Opportunity Screening** memorandum, June 12, 2025 (Sarah Lindgren, Thornfield Capital Partners — the internal sector framework).

Methodology: each document was read in full and cross-referenced against every other document. Every specific figure, date, party name, contract term, and identifier in the record was checked for consistency across documents and against the sector benchmarks in the Lindgren memo. Findings are stated with the exact values from the record, rated for severity, and carried through to consequence and recommended action. The sector benchmarks (reported EBITDA margin 10–15%; adjusted EBITDA margin 15–18%; maintenance capex 5–7% of revenue; DSO 45–55 days; NWC 10–14% of revenue; recurring regulatory costs $200K–$500K/year; company-level PFAS growth 30–50% after crossing $5M) are taken from the Lindgren memo and Thornfield's GreenWorks Remediation and Allied Waste Systems portfolio data. All dollar figures are in thousands or millions as labeled; the Exhibits are in $000s.

---

## 3. Transaction and Process Overview

Greenleaf is running a competitive three-phase sale of 100% of CES equity on behalf of founder/CEO Randall Oakes (68% holder) and other equity holders. Key terms from the Process Letter:

- **Phase I:** CIM and Exhibits distributed June 9, 2025; **IOIs due 5:00 PM Pacific, July 14, 2025** to Philip Tran (Greenleaf).
- **Phase II:** Management presentations (August 2025) at CES Portland HQ; virtual data room with audited/unaudited financials, material contracts, employee data, environmental permits and compliance records, insurance, and real estate documentation; site visits available.
- **Phase III:** Final binding bids; draft **stock purchase agreement** from Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP (seller counsel) distributed for markup; **target signing/closing Q4 2025.**
- **IOI must address (8 items):** (1) EV range on a cash-free/debt-free basis with normalized NWC, specifying trailing vs. projected and the implied EBITDA multiple; (2) transaction structure, including treatment of existing indebtedness, the HQ real estate owned by Mr. Oakes and leased to the Company, and any rollover/earnout; (3) financing sources; (4) remaining diligence and timeline; (5) post-close management/retention and accommodation of Oakes's 18–24 month transition; (6) approvals and timeline to close; (7) relevant sector experience; (8) contacts.
- **No direct contact** with CES management, employees, customers, suppliers, or Cascadia Commercial Bank; all communication through Greenleaf.
- Seller counsel: Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP. Auditor: Ridgeline Accounting Group, PC. Existing indebtedness with Cascadia Commercial Bank is "expected to be refinanced or repaid at closing."

CES is a Delaware C-corporation (EIN 93-1247856), founded 2009, HQ at 4200 Industrial Way, Portland, OR 97218, with offices in Seattle, WA and Boise, ID, operating across Oregon, Washington, Idaho, and Northern California. FY2024 revenue $87.3 million; 412 employees; 150+ active customers.

---

## 4. Company and Investment Thesis (Balanced View)

**Genuine strengths (to be preserved in the IC narrative):**

- **Integrated, 16-year platform.** Three complementary segments — Environmental Remediation ($48.2M, 55.2%), Industrial Cleaning ($27.8M, 31.8%), Emergency Response ($11.3M, 12.9%) — with cross-selling and a single-source value proposition for industrial customers.
- **Real regulatory moat.** Oregon DEQ Contractor License ENV-2009-04821; hazardous waste transporter permits in all four states (HWT-OR-2009-3847, HWT-WA-2015-6192, HWT-ID-2018-2204, HWT-CA-2019-8831); EPA Generator ID ORD-093124785; licensed PEs required for remediation sign-off. These credentials take years to assemble and are a genuine barrier to entry.
- **Reported economics are within sector norms.** Reported FY2024 EBITDA margin of 13.9% sits inside the 10–15% sector range, indicating a reasonably well-managed operation (not a turnaround).
- **Genuine growth, even discounting PFAS.** Core industrial cleaning has grown ~11–15% annually; the 12.5% headline CAGR is real, even if PFAS-flattered (see Finding R-4).
- **Safety and qualifications.** EMR of 0.87 (better than industry average); ISNetworld and Avetta qualifications required for refinery access.
- **Backlog and retention.** $42.6 million contracted backlog; stated 90% MSA renewal rate; no top-10 customer lost in five years (subject to verification — see Finding C-5).
- **Sector tailwinds and fund fit.** PFAS regulation, state enforcement, and infrastructure spend are durable demand drivers; CES fits Fund III's industrial/environmental services focus, and Thornfield's GreenWorks and Allied Waste experience provides direct operating playbooks.

**The thesis depends on three things that the sell-side materials do not yet substantiate:** (i) PFAS revenue scaling to $14.0 million in FY2025 from a $6.2 million base with no disclosed contracted backlog; (ii) renewal of the PNR MSA (21.4% of revenue) that expires before close; and (iii) a clean transition of founder-led customer relationships and PE sign-off capacity. The findings below quantify the risk in each.

---

## 5. Sector Benchmark Framework (from the Lindgren Memo)

| Metric | CES (per CIM) | Sector benchmark | Preliminary observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue growth CAGR (FY21–24) | 12.5% | 5–10% | Above average; partly PFAS-driven |
| Reported EBITDA margin | 13.9% ($12.1M) | 10–15% | Within range |
| CIM Adjusted EBITDA margin | 19.6% ($17.1M) | 15–18% adjusted | High; adjustments warrant scrutiny |
| Maintenance capex (% of revenue) | 3.4% ($3.0M) | 5–7% | Below sector range; likely understated |
| CIM-stated DSO | 52 days | 45–55 days | Appears in range; does not reconcile to balance sheet |
| Regulatory cost treatment | Full $1.2M add-back | $200K–$500K ongoing run-rate | Likely overstated as non-recurring |
| PFAS growth (FY24→FY25E) | 125.8% | 30–50% (company-level) | Significantly above achievable range |

Thornfield portfolio corroboration: GreenWorks Remediation (Fund II, 2019) — sell-side maintenance capex understated at 3.5% vs. 6.2% actual; a $380K "one-time" regulatory add-back proved partly recurring at ~$275K/year; an emerging service line projected at 80%+ growth delivered ~35%; a key project manager departed within six months of close. Allied Waste Systems (Fund II, 2021) — recurring regulatory costs ~$310K/year (one spike year $840K); maintenance capex ~5.8% of revenue; customer concentration required active post-close diversification; multi-state PE licensing created staffing bottlenecks. These patterns map directly onto the CES findings below.

---

## 6. Findings — Financial Statement Integrity (Cross-Document Reconciliation)

These findings are the foundation for the entire QoE: the sell-side financials do not reconcile across the CIM and the Exhibits, and the Exhibits do not internally tie. No reliance should be placed on sell-side EBITDA or net income until a QoE provider establishes the audited baseline.

### F-1. CIM income statement does not reconcile to the Exhibits (HIGH)
The CIM's Appendix A "Condensed Income Statement" and the Exhibits "Income Statement" sheet report materially different profitability for the same audited periods. FY2024 comparison:

| Line ($M) | CIM Appendix A | Exhibits (xlsx) | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Revenue | 87.3 | 87.3 | 0.0 |
| Cost of Revenue | (57.8) | (58.5) | (0.7) |
| Gross Profit | 29.5 | 28.8 | (0.7) |
| Gross Margin | 33.8% | 33.0% | (0.8 pts) |
| Operating Income (EBIT) | 7.8 | 11.1 | +3.3 |
| Pre-Tax Income | 6.7 | 9.0 | +2.3 |
| Net Income | 5.0 | 6.7 | +1.7 |

The CIM understates gross profit by ~$0.7 million and net income by $1.7 million (34%) relative to the Exhibits. The CIM's structure is internally consistent (EBITDA $12.1M − D&A $4.3M = Operating Income $7.8M), but it does not match the Exhibits. **Consequence:** the buyer cannot take either document at face value; the audited financials must be reconciled to a single set of numbers before any EBITDA or FCF analysis is credible. **Action:** engage QoE provider immediately; request the audited financial statements and lead schedules directly.

### F-2. Exhibits income statement does not internally tie (HIGH)
The Exhibits' own income statement is internally inconsistent in every historical year: Operating Income (EBIT) + D&A ≠ Reported EBITDA.

| ($M) | FY2021 | FY2022 | FY2023 | FY2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Income (EBIT) | 5.7 | 6.8 | 9.1 | 11.1 |
| + D&A | 3.2 | 3.5 | 3.8 | 4.2 |
| = Implied EBITDA | 8.9 | 10.3 | 12.9 | 15.3 |
| Reported EBITDA (as shown) | 7.4 | 8.6 | 10.3 | 12.1 |
| **Gap** | **1.5** | **1.7** | **2.6** | **3.2** |

The gap grows from $1.5 million to $3.2 million. This indicates either (a) D&A is already embedded in operating expenses (so "Operating Income (EBIT)" is mislabeled and is actually post-D&A, making Reported EBITDA understated), or (b) Reported EBITDA is computed on a different basis than the operating-income build. **Consequence:** the headline "Reported EBITDA $12.1 million" — the anchor of the entire valuation — is not derivable from the Exhibits' own operating-income and D&A lines. **Action:** QoE must establish the correct EBITDA definition and reconcile to the audit.

### F-3. CIM balance sheet does not reconcile to the Exhibits (HIGH)
The CIM's balance sheet (Section VI.D and Appendix A) differs materially from the Exhibits' Balance Sheet sheet as of December 31, 2024:

| ($M) | CIM | Exhibits | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cash & equivalents | 2.3 | 4.1 | +1.8 |
| Total current assets | 24.0 | 25.1 | +1.1 |
| PP&E, net | 18.7 | 21.3 | +2.6 |
| Goodwill & intangibles | (not shown) | 2.5 | +2.5 |
| Other non-current assets | 3.4 | 1.1 | (2.3) |
| **Total assets** | **46.1** | **50.0** | **+3.9** |
| Accounts payable | 5.4 | 6.1 | +0.7 |
| Total current liabilities | 12.2 | 13.3 | +1.1 |
| Total liabilities | 29.0 | 31.2 | +2.2 |
| Stockholders' equity | 17.1 | 18.8 | +1.7 |

The Exhibits' balance sheet ties to its cash-flow statement (PP&E rolls forward with $7.2M capex, $4.2M D&A, $0.3M disposals), so the Exhibits appear internally consistent while the CIM balance sheet does not match. **Consequence:** net debt, NWC, and the equity bridge all change depending on which balance sheet is used (cash differs by $1.8 million; PP&E by $2.6 million). **Action:** reconcile to the audited balance sheet; use the audited figures for the NWC peg and net-debt calculation.

### F-4. $2.5 million goodwill & intangibles implies an undisclosed acquisition (HIGH)
The Exhibits show $2.5 million of "Goodwill & Intangible Assets," constant from FY2021 through FY2024. The CIM's narrative is entirely organic — CES "commenced operations in 2009," "has grown its geographic footprint alongside its service capabilities," and presents tuck-in acquisitions only as a **future** "Growth Opportunity" ("CES is well-positioned to serve as an acquisition platform"). No historical acquisition is disclosed anywhere in the CIM. **Consequence:** (a) the 12.5% revenue CAGR may be partly inorganic, not purely organic as implied; (b) there may be an undisclosed acquired entity with its own integration, retention, or contingent-liability profile; (c) the "platform for tuck-ins" pitch is weakened if CES has already done (and not disclosed) a deal. **Action:** request full acquisition/divestiture history for the past 10 years, the nature of the $2.5 million goodwill, any related earnouts or indemnities, and revenue split organic vs. inorganic for FY2021–FY2024.

### F-5. CIM cash-flow figures are lower than the Exhibits (MEDIUM)
The CIM's Appendix A cash-flow summary understates both CFO and FCF relative to the Exhibits:

| ($M) | CIM FY24 | Exhibits FY24 |
|---|---|---|
| Cash from operations | 9.2 | 10.0 |
| Free cash flow | 2.0 | 2.8 |

The Exhibits' CFO includes $0.3 million of phantom-unit stock-based compensation and detailed working-capital movements the CIM condenses away. **Consequence:** the CIM is, if anything, conservative on cash flow — but FCF is weak under either presentation (see Finding K-2). **Action:** use the Exhibits' cash-flow detail (which ties to the balance sheet) for FCF analysis.

### F-6. Segment revenue differs between CIM and Exhibits in prior years (MEDIUM)
Segment revenue for FY2021–FY2023 differs between the CIM and the Exhibits (totals reconcile):

## 7. Findings — Quality of Earnings and Adjusted EBITDA

The seller's FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA bridge adds $5.0 million (29% of the $17.1 million total) to reported EBITDA of $12.1 million. Each adjustment is scrutinized below.

### Q-1. Aggregate adjustments are aggressive and several are overstated (HIGH)
$5.0 million of add-backs on $12.1 million of reported EBITDA is high for this sector (the Lindgren memo flags adjusted margins above 18–20% as warranting scrutiny; sector adjusted range is 15–18%). At least four of the six adjustments are overstated or misclassified (Q-2 through Q-5). A buyer-defensible Adjusted EBITDA is approximately **$14.1 million–$15.2 million** (central ~$14.6 million), roughly $1.9 million–$3.0 million below the seller's $17.1 million. At 8.0x–10.0x, that is **$15 million–$30 million of enterprise value** at stake in the bridge alone.

### Q-2. Owner-compensation normalization ($1.9M) overstates near-term savings (HIGH)
The CIM adds back $1.9 million, computed as Randall Oakes's total FY2024 compensation of $2.4 million (salary, bonus, benefits, and "personal expenses allocated to the Company") less an assumed replacement-CEO cost of $0.5 million. Problems: (a) the $2.4 million explicitly includes **personal expenses allocated to the Company** — these are a true add-back, but the replacement-CEO salary of $0.5 million is a real, ongoing cash cost that persists; (b) Oakes intends to remain for 18–24 months post-close in a transitional/advisory capacity, so the buyer will likely pay **both** Oakes (transition compensation) **and** a new CEO during the overlap, eroding the near-term saving; (c) the $0.5 million replacement assumption is unverified. **Defensible run-rate add-back:** approximately $1.0 million–$1.4 million (personal expenses plus excess owner cash comp above a market CEO package), a reduction of ~$0.5 million–$0.9 million. **Action:** QoE to split the $2.4 million into true personal/non-business expenses vs. compensation; confirm replacement-CEO cost; model transition overlap into year-one projections.

### Q-3. Related-party lease add-back ($0.8M) contradicts the CIM's own market comparable (HIGH)
CES leases its HQ at 4200 Industrial Way from Mr. Oakes personally at $1.4 million/year; the CIM normalizes to a $0.6 million "market" rate and adds back $0.8 million. **But the CIM's own Section IV.C states that "specialized environmental staging facilities commanding rates of $1.0 million to $1.2 million annually for properties of comparable acreage and infrastructure."** The HQ is precisely such a facility — a purpose-built environmental operations center with a permitted wash rack, secondary containment, roll-off staging, and soil stockpile areas (Section VIII.A). Using generic "industrial yard space" at $0.6 million to justify the add-back is inconsistent with the CIM's own comparable for the property's actual specialized use. **Defensible add-back:** approximately $0.2 million–$0.4 million (the excess of $1.4 million over a $1.0–$1.2 million specialized-facility rate), a reduction of ~$0.4 million–$0.6 million. Further, post-close the buyer must either renegotiate the lease with a departing Oakes (who may seek to monetize the real estate) or purchase the property at fair market value — in which case the rent add-back is irrelevant and replaced by owned-asset depreciation. **Action:** obtain an independent MAI appraisal of the HQ as a specialized environmental facility; structure the real estate as a separate purchase or a market lease as a closing condition (see Finding RE-1).

### Q-4. DEQ settlement appears double-counted in the $1.2M regulatory add-back (HIGH)
The CIM adds back $1.2 million for the August 14, 2024 Oregon DEQ matter ($875,000 settlement + $325,000 legal fees). The Exhibits, however, show "Other Income / (Expense)" of $(875) in FY2024 — i.e., the $875,000 settlement is classified **below operating income**, where it reduces pre-tax income but **does not reduce EBITDA**. If the settlement is below the line, it was never subtracted from EBITDA, so adding it back inflates Adjusted EBITDA by $875,000. Only the $325,000 of legal fees (presumably in operating expenses) would be a legitimate EBITDA add-back. **Consequence:** potential $875,000 overstatement of Adjusted EBITDA — at 8.0x–10.0x, $7.0 million–$8.8 million of enterprise value. **Action:** QoE to confirm the income-statement classification of the $875,000 settlement and the $325,000 legal fees against the audited financials; recompute the add-back on the correct basis.

### Q-5. Regulatory costs are treated as fully non-recurring against sector norms (HIGH)
Independent of the classification issue (Q-4), treating the full $1.2 million as "one-time" conflicts with sector reality. Per the Lindgren memo and Thornfield portfolio data, recurring regulatory and compliance costs for a company of CES's size run **$200,000–$500,000 per year** (GreenWorks: a $380K "one-time" add-back proved recurring at ~$275K/year; Allied: ~$310K/year recurring with a $840K spike year). QoE best practice is to normalize to an ongoing run-rate and add back only the excess. If a $300,000 run-rate is appropriate, the defensible add-back is the excess over $300,000 — not the full $1.2 million. Combined with Q-4, the defensible regulatory add-back is likely **$0.3 million–$0.7 million**, a reduction of ~$0.5 million–$0.9 million versus the seller's $1.2 million. **Action:** request CES's complete regulatory enforcement history for the past 10 years; establish an ongoing regulatory cost run-rate; add back only the excess.

### Q-6. Phantom-unit SBC ($0.3M) is a real cash cost at closing, not a run-rate add-back (HIGH)
The CIM adds back $0.3 million of non-cash phantom-unit stock-based compensation and states that "all outstanding phantom units are expected to be settled in cash at closing." Non-cash SBC is a standard EBITDA add-back, but here the units convert to **cash at closing** — a real use of cash that reduces net proceeds to the buyer. The total cash settlement liability is **not disclosed**. **Consequence:** (a) the $0.3 million should be removed from run-rate Adjusted EBITDA (it is real compensation, just non-cash until settlement); (b) the total settlement amount is a net-debt-like item / transaction expense that must be quantified and deducted in the equity bridge. **Action:** request the phantom-unit plan document, outstanding unit count, strike/value formula, and the estimated cash settlement at closing; model it as a closing use of funds.

### Q-7. Adjusted EBITDA CAGR of 20.8% is inflated by non-recurring add-backs (MEDIUM)
The CIM (Appendix B) states Adjusted EBITDA grew at a 20.8% CAGR (FY2021 $9.7M → FY2024 $17.1M), "outpacing revenue growth." Reported EBITDA CAGR is 17.8% ($7.4M → $12.1M). The gap is driven by add-backs that grew from $2.3 million (FY2021) to $5.0 million (FY2024), with the $1.2 million regulatory add-back appearing only in FY2024. On a like-for-like basis (excluding one-time regulatory and relocation items), margin expansion is more modest. **Action:** present reported EBITDA growth alongside adjusted in the IC materials; do not headline the 20.8% figure.

### Q-8. Projections remove owner comp without a replacement CEO and retain the above-market lease (HIGH)
The Exhibits' projected income statement (FY2025E–FY2027E) sets Owner Compensation (R. Oakes) to **$0** in every projection year — i.e., it removes the $2.4 million of owner comp entirely **without adding any replacement-CEO cost** — while retaining the Related-Party Lease Expense at the full $1,400 (above-market). This is inconsistent with the Adjusted EBITDA bridge, which assumes a $0.5 million replacement CEO and normalizes the lease to $0.6 million. The projections therefore understate operating expenses (no CEO) in one place and overstate them (full above-market rent) in another, in a way that does not net to the bridge. **Consequence:** the projected Adjusted EBITDA of $19.7M / $23.5M / $26.7M and the 20.0%→21.5% margin path are not built on a consistent cost base. **Action:** rebuild a clean projection set: include a market replacement-CEO cost from year one; normalize the lease (or assume property purchase); exclude one-time items; re-derive Adjusted EBITDA.

### Q-9. Projected margin expansion to 21.5% relies on aggressive assumptions (MEDIUM)
The margin path from 19.6% (FY2024A) to 21.5% (FY2027E) is attributed to operating leverage, a PFAS mix shift to above-average margins, and cost discipline. But it also relies on the $0 CEO cost (Q-8) and on PFAS hitting $14.0M/$22.0M/$27.0M (Findings R-1, R-2). With a real replacement CEO, a partially normalized lease, and a moderated PFAS ramp, the margin path is lower and the "expanding margins" narrative weakens. **Action:** stress-test margins under a moderated-PFAS / full-CEO-cost scenario.

## 8. Findings — Revenue and Growth Quality

### R-1. PFAS projection (125.8% FY2025 growth) is ~3x the achievable company-level rate (HIGH)
The CIM projects PFAS revenue of $14.0 million in FY2025E (125.8% growth from $6.2M) and $22.0 million in FY2026E (57.1%). The Lindgren memo concludes that company-level PFAS growth typically moderates to **30–50% annually once a practice crosses the $5 million threshold**, constrained by (a) licensed-engineer and trained-technician availability, (b) capital for specialized treatment equipment, (c) larger national entrants, and (d) the pace of enforcement that actually triggers **funded** remediation (vs. investigation/assessment). Thornfield's direct analog — GreenWorks' vapor intrusion practice — was projected at 80%+ growth in years two and three and delivered ~35% once pent-up demand was worked through. **Consequence:** if PFAS reaches $9–10 million in FY2025 rather than $14 million, the revenue trajectory and the margin expansion both weaken materially (PFAS is presented as above-average margin). **Action:** request the contracted PFAS backlog breakout (signed contracts vs. pipeline) supporting the $14.0M FY2025E figure; underwrite PFAS at 35–50% growth, not 125.8%.

### R-2. The growth thesis is ~64% PFAS-dependent (HIGH)
Per the Exhibits, PFAS drives $15.8 million of the $24.7 million incremental revenue from FY2024 to FY2026E — i.e., **64.0% of incremental growth** — and PFAS rises from 7.1% of total revenue (FY2024) to 21.8% (FY2027E). The entire platform growth story therefore rests on a service line launched in 2022 that is regulatory-dependent, capital-intensive, PE-dependent, and facing larger national competitors. **Consequence:** a PFAS shortfall cascades into revenue, margin, and the multiple the buyer can underwrite. **Action:** build the base case on core-segment growth (industrial cleaning, traditional remediation, emergency response) with PFAS as upside; consider an earnout tied to PFAS revenue.

### R-3. Contracted PFAS backlog is not disclosed (HIGH)
Neither the CIM nor the Backlog Report quantifies PFAS-specific contracted backlog. The Backlog Report notes only that "a significant portion of the Remediation pipeline relates to PFAS-related opportunities" (Remediation backlog $26.8M; Remediation pipeline $19.2M — PFAS is an undisclosed subset). Given that PFAS is 64% of incremental growth and projected to more than double in one year, the absence of a signed-contract breakout is a critical gap. **Action:** demand a PFAS-specific backlog and pipeline schedule (signed vs. awarded vs. pipeline, by customer and expected timing) as a Phase II gating item.

### R-4. Backlog coverage of the FY2025 projection is thin (HIGH)
Contracted backlog is $42.6 million, of which $33.0 million (77.5%) converts in FY2025 (H1 $18.2M + H2 $14.8M). FY2025E revenue is $98.5 million. **Only 33.5% of FY2025E revenue ($33.0M) is supported by contracted backlog.** The $31.4 million pipeline at a 35–40% historical win rate yields ~$11.0–$12.6 million of expected additional work. Contracted backlog plus expected pipeline yield therefore covers only ~$44–$46 million, leaving **~$53–$55 million (≈55% of FY2025E revenue) to be sourced from new business not yet in backlog or pipeline.** **Consequence:** the FY2025 projection relies heavily on in-year business development not currently visible; this is a projection-execution risk, not just a PFAS risk. **Action:** request a monthly/quarterly book-to-bill and proposal-submission trend; underwrite FY2025 revenue closer to the contracted-plus-yield figure unless pipeline conversion is demonstrated.

### R-5. CIM overstates "revenue visibility" by including the full pipeline at 100% (MEDIUM)
The CIM presents "total potential revenue visibility of $74.0 million" (backlog $42.6M + pipeline $31.4M). But the pipeline is un-awarded proposals; at a 35–40% win rate, only ~$11.0–$12.6 million is expected to convert. Including the full $31.4 million overstates visibility by ~$18.8–$20.4 million. **Action:** present visibility as contracted backlog plus risked pipeline in IC materials.

### R-6. Underlying organic growth ex-PFAS is ~6–8% in the largest segment (MEDIUM)
Using the Exhibits' "Traditional Remediation" line (Remediation ex-PFAS): $34.8M (FY21) → $37.0M (FY22) → $39.1M (FY23) → $42.0M (FY24), i.e., +6.3%, +5.7%, +7.4% — well below the 12.5% headline CAGR and within the sector's 5–10% range. Industrial cleaning grew ~11–15%; emergency response was volatile (see R-7). The 12.5% headline is flattered by PFAS (0 → $6.2M) and emergency-response variability. **Consequence:** a buyer underwriting "12.5% growth" is underwriting the PFAS ramp; core growth is mid-single to low-double digits. **Action:** present segment-level growth ex-PFAS in the IC model.

### R-7. Emergency response revenue is volatile and not underwriteable (MEDIUM)
Emergency response (12.9% of revenue) is "inherently variable — driven by unpredictable spill events." Annual growth: +17.6% (FY22), +5.7% (FY23), +22.8% (FY24). The FY2024 22.8% jump is unlikely to repeat; the projections assume +4.4% (FY25E), +10.2% (FY26E), +15.4% (FY27E). **Action:** underwrite emergency response flat to modest growth; do not give credit for the FY2024 spike.

### R-8. Gross margin expansion is modest and the CIM overstates it (MEDIUM)
Gross margin expanded from 31.0% to 33.0% (Exhibits) / 31.9% to 33.8% (CIM) over FY2021–FY2024 — roughly 2 points over four years. The CIM overstates gross margin by ~0.8–1.0 points versus the Exhibits in every year. The jump from 13.9% reported EBITDA margin to 19.6% adjusted margin is driven by add-backs, not by gross-margin expansion. **Action:** use the Exhibits' gross-margin figures; do not attribute the adjusted-margin level to operations.

### C-1. PNR (21.4% of revenue) MSA expires March 31, 2025 — before close (HIGH)
Pacific Northwest Refining Co. is the largest customer ($18.7M, 21.4% of FY2024 revenue), described as "strong and deeply embedded" with CES personnel on-site near-daily at PNR's Portland and Anacortes facilities. The PNR master service agreement **expires March 31, 2025** — six to nine months before the expected Q4 2025 close. **Consequence:** the buyer inherits its largest customer with **no current MSA in place** at closing; renewal is not guaranteed and the relationship, however embedded, is not contractually secured past the IOI date. **Action:** (i) request current renewal/extension status and any term sheet; (ii) make PNR renewal a specific diligence item and consider a closing condition, escrow, or earnout tied to PNR revenue; (iii) assess replacement-revenue capacity if PNR is lost.

### C-2. Top-10 customer identities disagree between the CIM and the Backlog Report (HIGH)
The CIM's Top-10 table and the Backlog Report's Top-10 table list **completely different customers for ranks #6–#10**, despite identical revenue amounts ($3.8M / $3.6M / $3.2M / $2.9M / $2.7M) and identical subtotals (Top 5 $45.8M; #6–#10 $16.2M; Top 10 $62.0M):

The identical dollar amounts and subtotals, attached to two different sets of names (and a near-identical set of contract-expiration dates), indicate that one document's customer identities are incorrect. **Consequence:** customer-concentration analysis cannot be relied upon until reconciled; this is a data-integrity red flag that extends to concern about the reliability of the broader data room. **Action:** request the authoritative customer list (with revenue, segment, contract type, and expiration) reconciled to the general ledger; reconcile both documents to it.

### C-3. Segments served disagree for three of the top five customers (HIGH)

The CIM narrative (Section V.A) and the Backlog Report table give different relationship-start years:

### C-5. Geographic concentration is understated by the CIM (MEDIUM)
The CIM (Section II.C) states "approximately 75% of FY2024 revenue was generated in Oregon and Washington, with the remaining 25% derived from Idaho and Northern California." The Backlog Report (Appendix A) shows Oregon 54.5% + Washington 27.8% = **82.3%**, and Idaho 10.0% + Northern California 7.7% = 17.7%. **Oregon alone is 54.5% of revenue** (more than half), driven by PNR and Willamette Steel in the Portland area. **Consequence:** the CIM's "approximately 75%" understates Oregon/Washington concentration by ~7 points and overstates the newer markets; Oregon single-state concentration (>50%) is a risk the CIM obscures. **Action:** use the 82.3% / 54.5% figures; stress-test an Oregon-specific downturn.

### C-6. Sector diversification is overstated in the largest sector (MEDIUM)
The Backlog Report's sector mix shows Oil & Gas / Refining at 25.7% ($22.4M) — the largest sector. But PNR alone is $18.7M, i.e., **PNR is ~83% of the entire Oil & Gas / Refining sector** ($18.7M of $22.4M). Combined with C-1 (PNR MSA expiring pre-close), refining exposure is a concentrated, contractually expiring risk. **Action:** present sector mix net of PNR; quantify refining exposure ex-PNR.

### C-7. Multiple top-10 contracts expire in 2025–2026; renewal claims unverified (MEDIUM)
Beyond PNR (March 2025), Meridian Lumber & Paper's MSA expires December 31, 2025, and several #6–#10 contracts expire in 2026 (June, September, December). Willamette Steel (5.6%) is **month-to-month** with no contractual visibility. The Backlog Report claims a ~90% MSA renewal rate and "no top-10 customer lost in five years," but these must be tested against the specific near-term expirations. **Action:** request renewal status and any renewal negotiations for every top-10 contract expiring within 18 months of close; obtain customer reference calls (via Greenleaf) for PNR, ODOT, Columbia Basin, and Meridian.

### C-8. Willamette Steel is month-to-month (LOW/MEDIUM)
Willamette Steel Corp. ($4.9M, 5.6%) operates on a month-to-month arrangement with no stated expiration. While the Backlog Report frames this as "flexible," it provides no contractual revenue visibility for a top-five customer in a cyclical end market (steel). **Action:** confirm whether a longer-term arrangement is contemplated; assess steel-cycle exposure.

## 10. Findings — Capital Expenditures and Free Cash Flow

### K-1. Stated maintenance capex (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the sector floor (HIGH)
The CIM and Exhibits show maintenance capex of $3.0 million (3.4% of FY2024 revenue). The sector benchmark is **5–7% of revenue** ($4.4M–$6.1M for CES), corroborated by Thornfield's portfolio (GreenWorks 6.2%; Allied 5.8%). Critically, GreenWorks' sell-side CIM **also** understated maintenance capex at ~3.5%, with actual post-close maintenance capex of ~6.2% as deferred maintenance and aging equipment surfaced only after a fleet assessment. The CES fleet is 82+ major units, average age ~4.5 years, replacement value >$22 million; the Exhibits' "New Fleet Additions (Expansion)" line ($2.2M in FY2024) likely includes replacement units that should be classified as maintenance. **Consequence:** if true maintenance capex is ~$4.8M (5.5%) rather than $3.0M, the $1.8 million annual difference directly reduces free cash flow and the implied return. **Action:** commission an independent fleet age, condition, and remaining-useful-life assessment; reclassify growth vs. maintenance capex; re-underwrite FCF.

### K-2. Free cash flow is negligible on a normalized basis (HIGH)
Reported FCF is weak under either source: $2.0 million (CIM) or $2.8 million (Exhibits) in FY2024 against $12.1 million reported EBITDA — only ~16–23% EBITDA-to-FCF conversion. The compression is driven by (a) growth capex of $4.2 million (FY2024) required for the PFAS/fleet growth thesis and (b) a working-capital build (AR grew $2.1 million in FY2024 alone). **On a normalized maintenance-capex basis, FCF is roughly $0–$1 million:** Exhibits CFO $10.0M − normalized total capex (~$9.0M–$10.3M, i.e., $4.8–6.1M maintenance + $4.2M growth) ≈ $0–$1M, and could be negative at the high end of the maintenance range. **Consequence:** a buyer paying 8–10x EBITDA is paying a very high FCF multiple for EBITDA that does not currently convert to cash; leverage capacity (a lender underwrites to FCF, not EBITDA) and the equity-return profile are both constrained. **Action:** underwrite returns on normalized FCF, not EBITDA; size debt to FCF; model the capex required to support the PFAS ramp as a deduction from FCF.

### K-3. Fleet age and deferred-maintenance risk (MEDIUM)
Average fleet age ~4.5 years across 82+ units with >$22 million replacement value; at a ~7-year replacement cycle, steady-state fleet replacement alone is ~$3.1 million/year, versus the $1.7 million of fleet maintenance plus $0.65 million of equipment overhaul currently classified as maintenance. This is consistent with K-1 (maintenance understated) and the GreenWorks pattern (deferred maintenance surfaced post-close). **Action:** fleet condition assessment (K-1); request maintenance records and any deferred-maintenance backlog.

## 11. Findings — Working Capital

### W-1. Stated DSO of 52 days does not reconcile to the balance sheet (HIGH)
The CIM states DSO of 52 days. The balance sheet (both CIM and Exhibits) shows accounts receivable, net of $16.9 million on FY2024 revenue of $87.3 million: **$16.9M / $87.3M × 365 = ~71 days** (or ~66 days on average AR of ($14.8M + $16.9M)/2). Even using FY2023 AR of $14.8M gives ~62 days. None of these reconcile to 52 days (which would require AR of ~$12.5M). The stated 52-day figure is ~14–19 days above the implied balance-sheet DSO and at/above the 45–55 day sector ceiling. **Consequence:** collections are slower than represented; the working-capital base is larger than implied; the NWC peg and post-close WC funding requirement are affected. **Action:** QoE to compute DSO from the AR aging and trailing revenue; reconcile to the 52-day claim; build the NWC peg on actuals.

### W-2. Accounts receivable is growing faster than revenue (MEDIUM)
AR grew from $10.8M (CIM FY21) / $11.4M (Exhibits FY21) to $16.9M (FY24) — a CAGR of ~16.0% (Exhibits) — versus revenue CAGR of 12.5%. AR growth outpaced revenue growth in every year, and especially in FY2024 (AR +19.0% vs. revenue +14.7% per Exhibits). This is a working-capital drain that explains part of the weak FCF (K-2) and contradicts the "strong collection practices" narrative. **Action:** obtain the AR aging; identify any large past-due balances (especially PNR and government receivables); assess collectibility.

### W-3. Net working capital peg (MEDIUM)
NWC is $11.8 million (13.5% of revenue) under both the CIM and Exhibits (the components differ but the net reconciles), within the 10–14% sector range. The Process Letter requires a "normalized level of net working capital." If DSO is drifting up (W-1, W-2), the buyer will fund working-capital growth post-close to support the revenue plan. **Action:** define the NWC peg as a percentage of LTM revenue using a trailing 12-month average DSO; negotiate a target NWC with a surplus/deficit true-up at closing.
