# CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL MEMORANDUM
## Buy-Side CIM Analysis — Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. ("CES" / "Project Cascade")

**To:** Marcus Yuen (Partner, Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook (Managing Partner)
**From:** David Koh (Associate) **CC:** Sarah Lindgren (Vice President)
**Date:** June 25, 2025 **Re:** Buy-side analysis of the CES CIM and supporting materials — financial inconsistencies, risk factors, valuation, and IOI recommendations
**Process:** IOI deadline July 14, 2025; target close Q4 2025; stock purchase structure | **Buyer counsel:** Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP

## 1. Executive Summary

**Recommendation: Proceed to submit a non-binding Indication of Interest, but anchor valuation on a buyer-adjusted EBITDA materially below the seller's $17.1 million figure, and condition the bid on a structured earnout and a defined diligence gate.** CES is a genuine, well-positioned Pacific Northwest environmental services platform that fits Fund III's sector and geographic focus, and Thornfield's GreenWorks and Allied Waste experience is directly relevant. However, the sell-side materials contain a pattern of internal inconsistencies, aggressive EBITDA adjustments, a customer-concentration contract that expires before close, and a growth thesis almost entirely dependent on a two-year-old service line.

**Valuation view.** The CIM implies EV of **$136.8M–$171.0M** (8.0x–10.0x FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1M) and pushes the "higher end." After correcting the EBITDA bridge, **buyer-defensible FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA is ~$14.1M–$15.2M** (central ~$14.6M), implying EV of roughly **$113M–$152M at 8.0x–10.0x**, central case near **$126M–$137M (≈9.0x)**. I recommend an IOI EV range of approximately **$115M–$140M** (cash-free, debt-free, normalized NWC), with the upper portion delivered through an earnout tied to (i) PFAS revenue achievement against the $14.0M FY2025E target and (ii) renewal of the PNR MSA. Note that even the low end of the seller's range exceeds Fund III's stated $30M–$100M platform EV target; fund-fit and any co-invest requirement should be confirmed with the IC before the IOI.

**Top risks:** (1) Sell-side financials do not reconcile across documents (net income differs by $1.7M; total assets by $3.9M; the Exhibits' own income statement does not tie); (2) Adjusted EBITDA is 29% adjustments and several add-backs are overstated; (3) PNR (21.4% of revenue) MSA expires March 31, 2025 — before close; (4) the growth thesis is PFAS-dependent and aggressive (125.8% FY25 growth, ~64% of incremental growth); (5) FCF is negligible on a normalized capex basis (~$0–$1M); (6) key-person/PE-capacity risk (Oakes exits in 18–24 months; PE headcount just fell 3→2); (7) data integrity — the CIM and Backlog Report disagree on 5 of the top 10 customer names.

## 2. Documents Reviewed
Full Project Cascade set: (1) CIM; (2) Customer Summary & Backlog Report; (3) Financial Exhibits workbook (6 sheets); (4) Process Letter; (5) internal Lindgren sector memo. Each was read in full and cross-referenced against every other; every figure, date, name, and identifier was checked for consistency and against sector benchmarks.

## 3. Transaction & Process Overview
Three-phase competitive sale of 100% CES equity; IOIs due July 14, 2025 to Philip Tran (Greenleaf); management presentations August 2025; draft **stock purchase agreement** from Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP; target close Q4 2025. IOI must address 8 items (value, structure incl. HQ real estate, financing, diligence, management/retention incl. Oakes's 18–24 month transition, approvals, experience, contacts). No direct contact with CES/Cascadia.

## 4. Company & Investment Thesis (Balanced)
Genuine strengths: integrated 16-year platform (Remediation $48.2M/55.2%, Industrial Cleaning $27.8M/31.8%, Emergency Response $11.3M/12.9%); real regulatory moat (DEQ license, 4-state HWT permits, PEs); reported EBITDA margin 13.9% within sector norms; genuine growth even ex-PFAS; EMR 0.87; $42.6M backlog; fits Fund III. The thesis depends on three unsubstantiated items: PFAS scaling to $14.0M with no disclosed contracted backlog; PNR renewal pre-close; and a clean founder/PE transition.

## 5. Sector Benchmark Framework (from Lindgren memo)
Revenue CAGR 12.5% vs 5–10% (above avg, PFAS-driven); reported EBITDA 13.9% vs 10–15% (in range); CIM adjusted 19.6% vs 15–18% (high, scrutinize); maintenance capex 3.4% vs 5–7% (below floor); DSO 52 days vs 45–55 (doesn't reconcile); regulatory full $1.2M add-back vs $200–500K run-rate; PFAS 125.8% vs 30–50% company-level. Corroborated by GreenWorks (capex understated 3.5%→6.2%; $380K "one-time" recurring at $275K; emerging line 80%+ projected → ~35% actual; key PM departed <6mo) and Allied ($310K/yr recurring; 5.8% capex; concentration; PE bottlenecks).

## 6. Findings — Financial Statement Integrity

- **F-1 (HIGH) CIM income statement ≠ Exhibits.** FY2024: Gross Profit $29.5M (CIM) vs $28.8M (Exhibits); Operating Income $7.8M vs $11.1M; Pre-Tax $6.7M vs $9.0M; **Net Income $5.0M vs $6.7M — a $1.7M / 34% difference.** The CIM is internally consistent but does not match the Exhibits. QoE must establish the audited baseline.
- **F-2 (HIGH) Exhibits income statement does not internally tie.** Operating Income + D&A ≠ Reported EBITDA in every year; the gap grows $1.5M→$3.2M (FY21→FY24). The headline "Reported EBITDA $12.1M" is not derivable from the Exhibits' own operating-income and D&A lines.
- **F-3 (HIGH) CIM balance sheet ≠ Exhibits.** Total assets $46.1M (CIM) vs $50.0M (Exhibits) — $3.9M diff; PP&E $18.7M vs $21.3M; cash $2.3M vs $4.1M. The Exhibits tie to their cash-flow statement; the CIM does not. Net debt, NWC, and the equity bridge all shift.
- **F-4 (HIGH) $2.5M goodwill & intangibles (constant FY21–24) implies an undisclosed acquisition**, inconsistent with the CIM's organic-growth narrative; the 12.5% CAGR may be partly inorganic.
- **F-5 (MEDIUM)** CIM cash flow understates Exhibits (FCF $2.0M vs $2.8M FY24).
- **F-6 (MEDIUM)** Segment revenue differs CIM vs Exhibits in FY21–23 (totals match); Exhibits add a "Traditional Remediation" sub-line.
- **F-7 (LOW)** Current portion of LT debt $2.3M (CIM) vs $1.8M (Exhibits); confirm total $17.3M.

## 7. Findings — Quality of Earnings & Adjusted EBITDA

- **Q-1 (HIGH) Aggregate adjustments are 29% of Adj. EBITDA ($5.0M of $17.1M);** several are overstated. Buyer-defensible Adj. EBITDA ~$14.1–15.2M — $1.9–3.0M / $15–30M of EV at stake.
- **Q-2 (HIGH) Owner-comp normalization ($1.9M) overstates savings.** $2.4M includes "personal expenses allocated to the Company"; $0.5M replacement CEO is a real ongoing cost; 18–24 month transition means paying both Oakes and a new CEO. Defensible ~$1.0–1.4M.
- **Q-3 (HIGH) Related-party lease add-back ($0.8M) contradicts the CIM's own comparable.** CIM §IV.C says specialized environmental staging facilities command **$1.0–1.2M**, vs the $0.6M "industrial yard" rate used for the add-back. The HQ is precisely such a facility. Defensible ~$0.2–0.4M.
- **Q-4 (HIGH) DEQ settlement appears double-counted.** Exhibits show the $875K settlement below operating income (not in EBITDA), so adding back the full $1.2M inflates Adj. EBITDA by $875K; only the $325K legal fees are a legitimate add-back. ~$7.0–8.8M of EV.
- **Q-5 (HIGH) Regulatory costs treated as fully non-recurring vs sector norms.** Recurring run-rate $200–500K/yr (GreenWorks $275K/yr; Allied $310K/yr); only the excess should be added back. Defensible ~$0.3–0.7M.
- **Q-6 (HIGH) Phantom-unit SBC ($0.3M) is cash at closing** — a real cash cost, not a run-rate add-back; total settlement liability undisclosed.
- **Q-7 (MEDIUM)** Adj. EBITDA CAGR of 20.8% is inflated by non-recurring add-backs concentrated in FY24; reported CAGR is 17.8%.
- **Q-8 (HIGH) Projections set Owner Compensation to $0 with no replacement CEO and retain the full above-market lease** — inconsistent with the bridge assumptions.
- **Q-9 (MEDIUM)** Margin expansion to 21.5% relies on $0 CEO cost and the aggressive PFAS ramp.

## 8. Findings — Revenue & Growth Quality

- **R-1 (HIGH) PFAS 125.8% FY25 growth is ~3x the achievable company-level rate** (30–50% after crossing $5M; GreenWorks analog 80%+ projected → ~35% actual).
- **R-2 (HIGH) Growth thesis is ~64% PFAS-dependent** ($15.8M of $24.7M incremental FY24–26).
- **R-3 (HIGH) Contracted PFAS backlog not disclosed.**
- **R-4 (HIGH) Backlog coverage is thin: only $33.0M (33.5%) of FY25E $98.5M is contracted; ~55% of FY25 revenue is not yet visible** even after risked pipeline.
- **R-5 (MEDIUM)** CIM overstates "visibility" by including the full $31.4M pipeline (35–40% win rate → only ~$11–12.6M converts).
- **R-6 (MEDIUM) Underlying ex-PFAS growth is ~6–8% in the largest segment** (Traditional Remediation $34.8M→$42.0M); headline 12.5% is PFAS-flattered.
- **R-7 (MEDIUM) Emergency response is volatile** (annual growth 5.7%–22.8%); don't underwrite the FY24 spike.
- **R-8 (MEDIUM) Gross margin expansion is modest (~2pts/4yr) and CIM overstates it by ~1pt**; the 19.6% adjusted margin is from add-backs, not operations.
